text:
Riverside Neurology Clinic

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Amelia Rowe
Riverside Neurology Clinic
14 Brookside Way, Manchester M3 5HZ

Dear Dr Amelia Rowe,

Sarah Bennett, DOB: 22-07-1984, Hospital No: M942517 NHS No. 3849201756
21 Heather Close, Salford, M6 7LP

Summary/Diagnoses: History of seizures in the context of prior head injury; post-surgical history notable for temporal lobectomy with subsequent clinical improvement; ongoing cognitive recovery; vitamin D insufficiency; ex-smoker.

Medication: Valproate 500 mg twice daily. Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily. Omeprazole 20 mg daily.

Thank you for asking me to review Sarah Bennett in clinic today. She attended with her sister, who has been closely involved in her care since surgery. Overall, her condition is improving. She reports better sleep regularity, fewer morning headaches, and a noticeable increase in stamina on short walks. Since her temporal lobectomy, she feels her concentration has gradually improved, and she is now reading for 20–30 minutes most evenings without losing track. She has returned to light volunteering at a local charity shop two afternoons per week and is tolerating this well.

Seizures: She had no seizures for nearly a year following initiation of Valproate, then developed myoclonic jerks leading to three tonic seizure two Saturdays ago. These events were witnessed by her sister at home. There were brief myoclonic jerks over several minutes preceding a cluster culminating in the three tonic events, each lasting under a minute, with rapid orientation thereafter and no injury. There have been no further episodes since. She denies intercurrent infection, missed doses, or new medications; she had, however, slept poorly the previous two nights and consumed significant caffeine that morning. She continues to use a seizure diary app and will share entries with our team monthly.

Examination: Alert, oriented, conversational. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves normal. Tone and power full in all limbs, no pronator drift. Gait steady with slightly reduced right arm swing, which is longstanding. No focal ataxia. Weight stable at 66 kg; BP 126/74 mmHg; pulse 68 bpm, regular.

Investigations: Post-operative MRI last year showed expected changes without new lesions. Routine bloods in July were unremarkable including normal LFTs and platelets. Valproate level not previously checked as seizures were controlled; we will obtain a trough level today for reference.

Plan:
- Continue Valproate 500 mg twice daily; no dose change today given overall improvement and isolated recent cluster with identifiable sleep deprivation trigger.
- Trough Valproate level, FBC, U&E, LFTs, vitamin D today.
- Reinforce sleep hygiene and trigger avoidance; patient has agreed to reduce caffeine and maintain regular bedtimes.
- Continue seizure diary and share entries monthly via the patient portal.
- Safety: sister to supervise bathing on days of poor sleep; no driving in line with DVLA guidance.
- Follow-up in 4 months, sooner if further events occur.

Overall, I am encouraged by her functional gains since surgery and the extended period of seizure freedom on Valproate; with optimisation of sleep and routine monitoring, we anticipate continued improvement.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: She had no seizures for nearly a year following initiation of Valproate, then developed myoclonic jerks leading to three tonic seizure two Saturdays ago.
She had no seizures for nearly a year following initiation of Valproate, then developed myoclonic jerks leading to three tonic seizure two Saturdays ago

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis:
- Identify seizure details: The letter states she had no seizures for nearly a year after starting Valproate, then had a cluster with three tonic seizures two Saturdays ago, and none since.
- Seizure-free criterion: Not met (last seizure < 6 months ago), so we must calculate frequency.
- Timeframe approximation: "nearly a year" ≈ ~1 year.
- Count: 3 seizures within that ~1-year period.
- Therefore, frequency ≈ 3 per year. Although described as a cluster, selecting the highest frequency by event count gives 3 per year.
seizure_frequency_number: She had no seizures for nearly a year following initiation of Valproate, then developed myoclonic jerks leading to three tonic seizure two Saturdays ago.
reference: She had no seizures for nearly a year following initiation of Valproate, then developed myoclonic jerks leading to three tonic seizure two Saturdays ago
